title: China's UN Ambassador Calls for Urgent Reopening of Strait of Hormuz Ahead of Trump-Xi Summit
summary: China's UN Ambassador Fu Cong emphasized the urgent necessity of maintaining the US-Iran ceasefire and reopening the Strait of Hormuz, stating the waterway closure will feature prominently on the agenda of the upcoming Trump-Xi summit in Beijing.
contents:
## China Pushes for Strait of Hormuz Resolution

### Diplomatic Statement
China's UN Ambassador Fu Cong addressed reporters at UN headquarters in New York on May 1, declaring the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz an "urgent necessity" and confirming the issue will dominate the agenda of the upcoming Trump-Xi summit in Beijing.

### Strategic Positioning
Ambassador Fu framed US-China relations as transcending the Iran crisis, stating "the world stands big enough for both the US and China" and that bilateral relations "go beyond" the Hormuz issue. He emphasized that maintaining a "steady, sound and sustainable relationship" between Washington and Beijing serves the interests of "the people of the entire world."

### Economic Impact
The Strait of Hormuz has remained effectively closed for two months since the February 28 outbreak of the US-Israel war against Iran, choking off approximately 20 percent of global oil and gas supplies and an estimated one-third of world fertilizer supply. Global energy prices surged past $100 per barrel, with Brent crude briefly touching $126 per barrel. UN Secretary General Guterres warned that prolonged disruption through mid-year would reduce global growth, increase inflation, and push tens of millions into poverty.

### Maritime Freedom Construct
The United States launched a new international coalition dubbed the "Maritime Freedom Construct" (MFC) to restart shipping through the strait, while France and Britain separately organized a complementary coalition that would activate only after peace takes hold.

### Iran's Position
Iran's new Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei declared that Tehran would maintain control over the strait, stating that foreigners "have no place there except at the bottom of its waters." Iran's Foreign Ministry defended the closure as legitimate under international law given the ongoing conflict.
